coredata.dc:title: Nontoxic fluorescent carbon nanodot serving as a light conversion material in plant for UV light utilization
coredata.dc:creator: Sai, Liman
Liu, Siqi
Qian, Xuexue
Yu, Yahui
Xu, Xiaofeng
coredata.dc:description: In this study, water-soluble fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) were directly injected into the leaf of nicotiana tabacum. With the help of UV-to-blue light conversion nanomaterial, the photosynthetic rate of the leaf was improved 18% upon additional 6 W UV irradiation. The photostability and toxicity of different kinds of CNDs were discussed. The results showed that CNDs functionalized with NH2-groups on their surfaces could maintain good fluorescence in plant leaf, and CNDs with complex surface groups tended to have high toxicity to the plant. The NH2-functionalized CNDs with non-toxicity and good photostability were used as in vivo light conversion material for direct utilization of UV light in the solar energy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent carbon nanodots
Nanotoxicity
Photosynthesis
Light conversion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Treatment of nicotiana tabacum with CNDs solution
Photostability of the CNDs in plant
Toxic study of the CNDs in plant
CND-increased photosynthesis with additional UV supplement

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (1) COOH-CNDs, (b) NH2-CNDs, (c) PEI-CNDs and (d) LG-CNDs.
Typical XRD pattern (a) and Raman spectrum (b) of the carbon nanodots.

UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence emission spectra of (a) COOH-CND (b) NH2-CND (c) PEI-CND and (d) LG-CND. COOH-CND, NH2-CND and PEI-CND were excited at 365 nm, while LG-CNDs were excited at 290 nm.
FTIR spectra (a) and four kinds of CNDs with different surface groups.
Photograph of (a) NH2-CND and (b) PEI-CND in the leaves of nicotiana tabacum under 365 nm UV light one day after the injection.
Photoluminescence spectra of PEI-CND in solution and in the leaf of nicotiana tabacum under 365 nm excitation.
Photograph of four leaves exposed to different kinds of CNDs after 2 h, 20 h, and 2 days of injection.
Photograph of the equipment for photosynthesis measurement.

Responses of the leaf photosynthetic rate under LED white light (black curve) and with additional UV light (red curve) in the absence (b) and in the presence (b) of PEI-CNDs. Inset: Average photosynthetic rate during the recording time from 2 s to 46 s (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Nontoxic fluorescent carbon nanodot serving as a light conversion material in plant for UV light utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life and Environmental Sciences, Shanghai Normal University, Shanghai, 200234, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life and Environmental Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Shanghai Normal University, Guilin Road 100, Shanghai, 200234, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, water-soluble fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) were directly injected into the leaf of nicotiana tabacum. With the help of UV-to-blue light conversion nanomaterial, the photosynthetic rate of the leaf was improved 18% upon additional 6 W UV irradiation. The photostability and toxicity of different kinds of CNDs were discussed. The results showed that CNDs functionalized with NH2-groups on their surfaces could maintain good fluorescence in plant leaf, and CNDs with complex surface groups tended to have high toxicity to the plant. The NH2-functionalized CNDs with non-toxicity and good photostability were used as in vivo light conversion material for direct utilization of UV light in the solar energy.

ce:section.ce:para: The solar spectrum is extremely important for the growth of plants besides water and oxygen. In general, necessary irradiation light for plant exists in three regions from the solar spectrum: blue (400–500 nm), red (620–690 nm), and far red (739–735 nm), the ultraviolet (UV) and green components of the solar spectrum cannot be utilized by plants. Especially, UV radiation to the ground has increased because of the depletion of the ozone layer, which is caused by releasing of the chemical substances these years. Therefore, it will be useful to convert UV light into a band that can be absorbed by plant, which will not only reduce the damage of UV irradiation to the plant, but also promote plant growth.

Generally, the property of a light conversion material depends on its absorption (Abs) and photoluminescence (PL) bands. So far, various kinds of light conversion materials have been developed in recent years. They could successfully convert UV-light to blue [1], red [2,3] or far red [4] region. These materials were mostly used for fabrication of light-emitting diodes (LEDs) as artificial light sources, and have showed benefits on growth performance of plants.

However, the cost of the fabrication of LEDs brings energy consumption. It will be more convenient if UV component in the solar energy can be directly used by plants. Therefore, we have considered about the in vivo light conversion by the uptake of fluorescent nanomaterials in plants. The use of fluorescent probes in mammalian cells for bioimaging have been widely reported these years. However, few literatures are available discussing the application of these materials in plant system [5].

Generally, the biocompatibility and photostability of a certain kind of nanomaterial are two main points for it to be used in live plants. Phytotoxic studies about various kinds of nanomaterials have been reported in recent years. For example, plants were exposed to silica nanoparticles [6], carbon nanotube [7], quantum dots [8], graphene oxide [9], and the impact of these nanomaterials on ecotoxicology of the plants was discussed. Always, the ecotoxicological effect of most nanomaterials was hard to be avoided. In the case of quantum dots, it is probably due to the intrinsic toxicity of heavy metals in them. And for carbon nanotubes, organic groups on the surfaces of the nanoparticles may alter the nutrient uptake of the plant [7].

Carbon nanodots (CNDs), a new kind of fluorescent nanomaterial with size less than 10 nm, have showed remarkable attention these years due to their low cytotoxicity compared to traditional quantum dots. The biological toxicity of the CNDs to animals was reported to be very low even at a high concentration [10], and application of the CNDs in biological [11] and medical [12] field was widely investigated. Besides, they also showed advantage of water-solubility, chemical inertness and good biocompatibility, which made them a good choice to be used in live plants.

Due to the intrinsic optical property of aromatic structure in CNDs, they commonly showed blue photoluminescence upon excitation of UV light. This provides a potential application of them to be a light conversion material. Light with 400–520 nm wavelength is highly absorbed by the pigments (chlorophylls and carotenoids), and has strong effect on photosynthesis [13]. Terfa et al. reported an improvement of photosynthesis by increasing blue light from 5 to 20% [14]. Hogewoning et al. studied on cucumber and found the potential for photosynthesis in high light intensity increase with blue light increased up to 50% [15]. So it is reasonable to predict that, conversion of UV light into blue region may benefit to the photosynthesis improvement of plants.

In spite of the good ecological and optical properties of the CNDs, few reports have discussed the behavior of CNDs in live plants [16–18]. In this paper, blue-emitted carbon nanodot serving as an in vivo light conversion material was used for photosynthesis improvement. Four different kinds of CNDs were prepared, and their aqueous solution were injected to the leaves of nicotiana tabacum. We found great difference of the toxicity and luminescence behavior of the CNDs with different kind of surface groups. The results showed that, CNDs with amino groups showed both photostability and low toxicity in plant, and photosynthesis was improved 18% under illumination of a 6 W UV-lamp.

Citric acid, polyethylenimine (molecular weight 1800), glucose, ethylenediamine and ammonium hydroxide solution were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Reagents Company. β-lactoglobulin (molecular weight 18 kDa) was purchased from Sigma (USA). Mili-Q water was used as the solvent for all solutions. All the reagents were used without further purification.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was performed on a JEOL-2011 electron microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 KV. Sample for TEM was prepared by dropping CNDs solution onto a carbon-coated copper grid. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were measured on a Philips X'Pert diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Thermo-Nicolet transform infrared spectrometer (Nicolet/Nexus 670). Raman spectrum was measured with a Renishaw Raman system model 1000 spectrometer with radiation at 633 nm. Zeta potential of the CNDs were measured by a PSS NicompTM 380Zeta Potential/Particle Sizer (Santa Barbara, California). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a Lambda 950 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu RF-6310PC spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Columbia, MD, USA).
Four kinds of carbon nanodots (CNDs) with different functional groups were prepared via hydrothermal method. The synthetic process of the CNDs are described as follows:

CNDs with hydroxyl groups (named as COOH-CND) were prepared by means of microwave synthesis [19]. Typically, 2 g glucose was dissolved in 10 ml deionized water. Then 1 ml ammonia was diluted to 7 ml, and added to the above glucose solution under stirring. The mixed solution was subjected to microwave irradiation for 2 min at 120 °C.

CNDs with short-chain amino groups (named as NH2-CND) were prepared by means of microwave synthesis [11]. Typically, citric acid was dissolved in deionized water with concentration of 0.5 M. Then 665 μl ethylenediamine was added dropwise to a 20 ml above citric acid solution under stirring. The mixed solution was subjected to microwave irradiation at 150 °C for 10 min.

CNDs with long-chain amino groups (named as PEI-CND) were prepared according to previous report [20]. Typically, 0.75 g citric acid and 0.5 g polyethylenimine (PEI) were dissolved in 10 ml deionized water, and the mixed solution was treated by hydrothermal synthesis at 200 °C for 8 h.

CNDs with a variety of surface groups which derived from protein (named as LG-CND) were prepared according to our previous report [21]. Typically, 100 mg β-lactoglobulin was dissolved in 10 ml deionized water. Then another 10 ml water which contain 300 μl ethylenediamine was added dropwise to the above protein solution under stirring. The mixed solution was treated by hydrothermal synthesis at 200 °C for 7 h.

All the as-prepared CNDs were naturally cooled down to the room temperature and concentrated by rotary evaporation. Purification was done by dialysis (0.5 KD) for about 2 days by deionized water to remove the excess unreacted ions, and the obtained CNDs were stored in 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Treatment of nicotiana tabacum with CNDs solution
ce:section.ce:para: The exposure of the plant to nanomaterial was commonly achieved by immersing the plant seedlings in a diluted solution of nanoparticles. The state of seed germination and growth of roots were investigated after several days of cultivation. This kind of approach need a long period of time. In this paper, the diluted CNDs solution was directly injected into the leaves of the nicotiana tabacum. After one day of diffusion, the nanoparticles were uniformly dispersed throughout the treated leaf. And the toxic effect of different kind of CNDs was investigated by observing the change of the exposed leaves.

Measurements of photosynthesis was performed with a gas analyzer (LI6400XT, USA), and fitted with a UV penetrable leaf chamber. The leaf chamber was fed with CO2 and ambient air. Gas flow was set at 300 μmol s−1. Reference CO2 was set at 200 μmol mol−1. Block temperature was controlled at 25 °C. The light was set to 100 μmol m−2 s−1. A portion of a light-adapted leaf was clamped in the cuvette. Photosynthesis of the leaf was measured every 2 s for about 1 min.

TEM images of four kinds of CNDs are shown in Fig. 1 . As seen in the picture, all the CNDs showed good monodispersed distribution. The average sizes of the COOH-CNDs, NH2-CNDs and PEI-CNDs were about 2–3 nm, while the average size of the LG-CNDs was about 10 nm. This kind of difference maybe due to the slightly different formation process of LG-CNDs compared with other three kinds of the CNDs. Generally, the graphite structure in the carbon nanodots were formed by dehydration of amino groups and carboxyl groups of the precursor molecules. In the case of LG-CNDs, the dehydration process occurred between the peptide chains of the LG protein, and the sizes of LG-CNDs were mainly controlled by the original size of the protein molecule. While in the case of three other kinds of the CNDs, the dehydration process took place between small precursor molecules, and the sizes of the resulted CNDs were in the range of 2–3 nm, which was commonly found in most CNDs reported in the References

In this paper, the difference between the four kinds of CNDs were mainly their surface groups, and the crystal structure of these CNDs were similar. Typical XRD pattern and Raman spectrum of the CNDs are shown in Fig. 2 . In Fig. 2a, the broad diffractive peak of the CND at 18.09° revealed its amorphous nature [22], while the peak at 26.31° (0.34 nm) was attributed to highly disordered carbon, similar to d002 graphite lattice spacing [18]. In Fig. 2b, the peak at 1587 cm−1 (G band) corresponded to the E2g mode of the graphite and was related to the vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a 2D hexagonal lattice [23]. The high fluorescence may disturbed the Raman characterization [11].

UV-vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the four kinds of CNDs are shown in Fig. 3 . Generally, the two peaks in the UV–vis spectra of CNDs can be assigned to the n-π* transition (230–270 nm) and π-π* transition (300–350 nm), which were commonly found in carbon nanodots. In the PL spectra, the CNDs all showed blue emission, which originated from the emissive free zigzag sites [24]. In general, the emission peaks of the CNDs ranged from 360–480 nm [25], and most of the CNDs showed a blue emission at 430–460 nm. In our experiment, to obtain CNDs with various surface groups for comparation, LG protein was chosen as the precursor, and LG-CNDs were prepared according to the method reported in our previous work [21]. It is known that, the photoluminescence property of the CNDs depends largely on their surface states [26], and the emission peak of the LG-CNDs at 387 nm may be resulted from the complicated structure of the LG protein. The ultraviolet absorption and blue emission of the CNDs made them potential light conversion materials for plants.

FTIR spectroscopy are widely used to investigate the surface groups of nanoparticles. CNDs used in this work were functionalized with different kinds of surface groups. Usually, it could be predicted from the synthesis methods that, COOH-CND was mainly covered with carboxylate groups, NH2-CND and PEI-CND with less and more NH2-groups, while LG-CND covered with various kinds of surface groups such as OH, COOH and NH2, resulting from the complex structure of protein. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the peaks at 1630–1650 cm−1 and 1530–1580 cm−1 in all the curves could be assigned to the amide I CO [22] and NH [27] vibration, respectively, which confirmed the existence of amide bonds and NH2 groups, respectively. Peaks at –1700 cm-1 which represented the carboxylate groups could be found in COOHCND and LG-CND. FTIR data of the CNDs were in good consistent of the prediction of their surface groups.

For further characterization of the surface groups of the CNDs, zeta potential of four kinds of the CNDs were measured in different pH range. In general, a strong electronic repulsion between charged nanodots will result in a well dispersed solution. As shown in Fig. 4b, COOH-CNDs showed negative charge in the pH range of 6.7–9.5, and when the pH decreased to lower than 6.5, the CNDs tended to aggregate due to the protonation of the carboxyl groups and weak repulsion between the CNDs. In the case of NH2-CNDs and PEI-CNDs, they exhibited positive charge in the pH range of 6–8, and PEI-CNDs showed a faster increase of the zeta potential value with the decrease of pH, which may be resulted from the higher coverage of amino groups on their surfaces. LG-CNDs could remain well dispersed in water in a wide pH range from 4–11, and in Fig. 4b, they showed positive charge at pH 5.87, and negative charge when the pH was above 8. This kind of property may be resulted from the various kind of surface groups covered on the LG-CNDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photostability of the CNDs in plant
ce:section.ce:para: For efficient application of a fluorescent material in plants, it is important that its optical property should be maintained in plant. In this work, four kinds of CNDs were injected to the leaves of nicotiana tabacum. Interestingly, it was found that only NH2-CND and PEI-CND showed stable blue emission in plants. Fig. 5 showed the photograph of these two kinds of CNDs with NH2-groups in the leaves of nicotiana tabacum under UV light excitation. As seen in the picture, the nanoparticles uniformly diffused throughout the leaf and showed obvious blue emission. Fig. 6 showed the PL spectra of PEI-CND in solution and in leaf. It was found that the emission peak of the CNDs in leaf remained almost the same position with that of them in solution, which indicated good dispersity of the CNDs in leaf. On the contrary, the fluorescence of COOH-CND and LG-CND was completely quenched almost immediately after the injection of them into the leaf. The different optical properties of these CNDs in leaves were likely related to the surface groups of them, as the two NH2-functionalized CNDs showed same behavior. It was reported that, the photoluminescence of CNDs could be affected by the coordination of their surface groups with different ions or analytes [28,29]. The chemical process occurred around CNDs in plant is not clear till now, and the reason of this phenomenon will be further investigated in future work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxic study of the CNDs in plant
ce:section.ce:para: The toxicology of a material has to be well addressed before its in vivo application. Always, the behavior of the nanoparticles inside the plant system was complicated and unpredictable, and results of the experiments varied with the type of plants and materials. In this work, different toxicity of the CNDs to nicotiana tabacum could be recognized from the exposed leaves. Fig. 7 showed the photograph of the leaves containing different kind of CNDs. As the picture shows, the leaves with COOH-CND, NH2-CND and PEI-CND remained almost unchanged, while the leaf with LG-CND appeared to be seriously damaged. The results of this experiment showed high toxicity effect of CNDs prepared with LG protein, the reason may be due to the complex surface groups covered on them, which could cause strong interaction of the analytes in plants with the nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CND-increased photosynthesis with additional UV supplement
ce:section.ce:para: For consideration of both photostability and toxicity of the CNDs, NH2-functionalized CND could be a proper choice for our experiment. As the two kinds of NH2-functionalized CNDs showed similar behavior, only the data of PEI-CND were presented. For photosynthesis measurement, a hand-made shelf covered with black paper was used as a dark room (Fig. 8 a). A 365 nm UV lamp was used for supplemental UV irradiation (Fig. 8b). Fig. 8 showed the responses of photosynthetic rate of the original (Fig. 9 a) and exposed leaf (Fig. 9b) measured under LED white light (black curves) and with additional 6 W UV-light (red curves), respectively. The results showed that, with supplied additional 6 W UV-light, the photosynthetic rate of the exposed leaf was improved approximately 18%, while that of the original leaf remained almost the same. The improvement of the photosynthesis was likely due to the conversion of UV light to blue light by CNDs in the leaf, further evidence and investigation will be discussed in future paper.

In this paper, fluorescent carbon nanodot with good photostability and low toxicity was used as an in vivo light conversion material for improvement of the photosynthesis of nicotiana tabacum. CNDs functionalized with NH2-groups showed superiority to other two kinds of CNDs, indicating the important effect of surface groups. UV light in the solar energy could be changed to blue light utilizing the optical property of the CNDs, and the photosynthetic rate of the leaf loaded with CNDs was improved 18% with an additional 6 W UV lamp.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Shanghai Sailing Program (17YF1414000), and the Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai (No. 16ZR1424400), Shanghai Municipal Education Committee Key Laboratory of Molecular Imaging Probes and Sensors for Shanghai Universities.
